plainEnglish: Claude is Anthropic's AI assistant. You type instructions in normal English, and it replies with text, code, images, or by taking actions like reading your files. Think of it as a tireless analyst sitting next to you, available 24/7.
cxoAnalogy: An always-on analyst who is fast and literal — but needs a clear brief, the same way a contractor does.
examples: Ask Claude: 'Read this 30-page contract and tell me the top 5 risks in plain English.' It returns a summary in 30 seconds.
Ask Claude: 'Draft a friendly email to a customer apologizing for the late delivery and offering a 10% discount.' It writes the email; you tweak and send.

plainEnglish: Claude Code is the Claude app that runs on your laptop. Unlike chat-only Claude, this version can read files on your computer, edit them, and run commands you approve — useful when you want Claude to actually DO things, not just describe them.
cxoAnalogy: Claude with hands. It can open documents on your machine, change them, and run things — not just chat.
examples: Open Claude Code in your terminal, point it at a folder of spreadsheets, and say: 'Combine these into one master sheet with totals.' It does it.
Tell Claude Code: 'Read all my meeting notes from last week and write a summary file.' It scans the folder and creates the summary.

plainEnglish: Claude Cowork is the browser version of Claude — it lives at claude.ai. You use it for everyday chat, sharing conversations with your team, and quick tasks that don't need access to your laptop's files.
cxoAnalogy: Claude in the meeting room — visible to others on your team, accessible from any browser.
examples: Open claude.ai on your phone during a board meeting, paste a question, get an instant draft response to share.
Share a Claude conversation link with your COO so they can continue the thread you started.

plainEnglish: A prompt is the instruction you give Claude. The better your brief, the better the output. A vague prompt gets a vague answer; a specific prompt with context gets exactly what you wanted.
cxoAnalogy: A brief to a contractor: state the scope, audience, format, constraints, and what 'done' looks like.
examples: Bad prompt: 'Write a memo.' Good prompt: 'Write a 300-word memo to my CEO explaining why we should delay the launch by two weeks. Tone: confident, not apologetic.'
Bad prompt: 'Make a slide.' Good prompt: 'Make a 1-slide summary for a board audience that shows Q3 revenue, the variance to plan, and the top reason for the variance.'

plainEnglish: Context is everything Claude can 'see' during your conversation — your latest prompt, any files you attached, and the earlier messages in this chat. Claude has no memory of previous chats; each new conversation starts fresh unless you give it context.
cxoAnalogy: Background materials in a meeting — what Claude already has on the table to work with.
examples: Paste last quarter's board deck and say: 'Use this as context. Now write the Q4 version.' Claude matches your style and structure.
Attach a CSV file and ask: 'Use this data as context. Which 3 customers are most at risk of churning?' Claude reasons from the actual data, not generic advice.

plainEnglish: A model is a specific version of Claude. Bigger models think more carefully but cost more and respond slower; smaller models are faster and cheaper but less sharp on hard problems. You can pick which one you use.
cxoAnalogy: Senior vs. junior consultant. Use senior for complex strategy, junior for fast research and triage.
examples: Use the biggest model (Opus) when reviewing a complex 50-page contract — you want maximum care.
Use the smallest model (Haiku) when tagging 500 customer emails by topic — you want speed and low cost.

plainEnglish: Opus is Anthropic's most capable Claude model — the one to use when the problem is hard, multi-step, or high-stakes. It's slower and pricier than the others, but worth it when quality really matters.
cxoAnalogy: Your partner at the consulting firm. You don't put them on triage — you save them for the board meeting.
examples: Pricing strategy for a new SKU: ask Opus to weigh three options with trade-offs.
Drafting an investor letter that needs to land just right: Opus gives you a stronger first draft.

plainEnglish: Sonnet is the balanced Claude model — strong enough for almost any everyday task, faster than Opus, cheaper too. This is the right default for most CXO work.
cxoAnalogy: Your VP — handles the bulk of work, well. Solid all-rounder.
examples: Daily meeting prep, email triage, drafting SOPs — Sonnet handles all of these well.
Summarizing a 20-page market report into a 1-page brief: Sonnet is fast and accurate enough.

plainEnglish: Haiku is the fastest, cheapest Claude model. Use it for short, simple tasks where you need speed or you're running the same thing many times in a row.
cxoAnalogy: Your fastest analyst — perfect for triage and quick passes, not deep work.
examples: Auto-tagging 1,000 incoming support tickets by urgency: Haiku at scale.
Quick rewrites: 'make this sentence punchier' — instant with Haiku.

plainEnglish: A token is the unit Claude reads and writes in. Roughly, one token equals 3-4 characters of text — so 100 words is about 130 tokens. You pay per token in (your prompt) and per token out (Claude's response).
cxoAnalogy: Like billable minutes for a lawyer. Pay attention to long inputs and verbose outputs.
examples: Pasting an entire 300-page contract uses ~80,000 tokens. Pasting just the 5 relevant sections uses ~3,000. Same answer, 25× cheaper.
Asking for 'a 200-word summary' is cheaper than 'a comprehensive analysis' — and often more useful.

plainEnglish: MCP stands for Model Context Protocol. It's a shared standard that lets Claude (and other AI tools) plug into your apps — Gmail, Drive, Calendar, Figma, Salesforce, etc. Once an app speaks MCP, any AI that supports MCP can use it.
cxoAnalogy: The USB-C of AI integrations. Plug a tool in once, every MCP-compatible AI can use it.
examples: You install the Gmail MCP server. Now Claude can read and reply to your inbox without you copy-pasting.
Figma supports MCP. So Claude can open Figma files, see your designs, and propose edits.

plainEnglish: A connector is a specific MCP server you've turned on — Gmail, Slack, Notion, etc. Connectors give Claude permission to read or change data inside that app. You add or remove them with one click.
cxoAnalogy: A signed access pass for a specific building. The pass to Gmail doesn't open the door to Slack.
examples: Add the Calendar connector. Now Claude can see your meetings and propose times that don't conflict.
Remove the Salesforce connector. Claude can no longer touch your CRM until you add it back.

plainEnglish: A subagent is a child Claude session that handles a piece of a bigger task on its own — with its own scratch space and tools. You spin one up when you want to delegate a sub-task without losing focus on the main thread.
cxoAnalogy: Delegating a sub-task to a junior so the partner can keep focus on the big picture.
examples: Main Claude: 'Plan our 2027 strategy.' It spawns a subagent for 'do the competitive scan' and another for 'pull the financial baseline'. Each works independently, then reports back.
Coding example: one subagent writes tests while another writes the feature. Then they merge.

plainEnglish: A hook is a small script Claude Code runs automatically at certain moments — before it uses a tool, after it edits a file, before it commits to git, etc. Useful for enforcing rules without thinking about them.
cxoAnalogy: An office SOP — 'every time X happens, do Y.' Runs without anyone reminding it.
examples: A hook that runs your test suite automatically after any file change — so you catch bugs immediately.
A hook that blocks Claude from editing files in your '/secrets' folder — extra safety guardrail.

plainEnglish: A slash command is a shortcut you type into Claude Code starting with a '/' that triggers a specific routine — like /review, /summarize, /commit. Saves you from re-typing the same instruction every day.
cxoAnalogy: Speed dial. One keystroke triggers a complex routine you'd otherwise type out.
examples: Type '/review' and Claude reviews your last code change against your style guide.
Type '/board-prep' and Claude pulls together your monthly board-meeting brief in your preferred format.

plainEnglish: A worktree is a separate working copy of a project so two pieces of work can happen at the same time without stepping on each other. Mainly a developer concept — useful when you want Claude to try something experimental without touching your main version.
cxoAnalogy: A second copy of a contract you can mark up freely without messing up the original.
examples: You have a working app. Claude opens a worktree to try a risky refactor — your main version stays untouched. If it works, you merge; if not, you delete the worktree.
Two simultaneous experiments: one worktree for 'fancy redesign', one for 'small tweak'. Compare side by side.

plainEnglish: A skill is a reusable bundle of instructions and resources Claude follows when a relevant task comes up. Think of it as a saved playbook — write it once, Claude uses it forever after.
cxoAnalogy: A playbook your team can apply when the right situation arises. New hires read it on day one.
examples: A 'board-meeting-prep' skill that tells Claude exactly which docs to pull, in what order, with what summary format.
A 'customer-escalation' skill: when a customer complaint comes in, follow these 7 steps.

plainEnglish: Permissions are the rules about what Claude is allowed to read, write, or delete. You grant or revoke them per connector, per folder, even per command. Best practice: give the minimum that lets the work happen.
cxoAnalogy: Role-based access for employees. The intern doesn't get the master keys.
examples: Grant Claude read-only access to your /finance folder — it can analyze but never overwrite.
Block Claude from deleting any file without your one-click approval. Safety net for accidents.

plainEnglish: A hallucination is when Claude states something confidently that isn't true — invented sources, made-up numbers, fictional people. Less common with strong context and verification, but it happens. Always check facts that matter.
cxoAnalogy: An overconfident new hire winging an answer. Verify before you ship.
examples: Claude cites a Harvard Business Review article from 2024 that doesn't exist. Catch it by asking: 'Give me the URL.'
Claude says your churn last quarter was 4.2% — but you never told it the real number. Verify before quoting it.

plainEnglish: Temperature is a dial that controls how creative versus how predictable Claude's responses are. Low = consistent, conservative, repeatable. High = varied, creative, surprising. Most CXO work uses low-to-medium.
cxoAnalogy: Brainstorm mode vs. board-paper mode.
examples: Low temperature for legal redlines — you want the same careful answer every time.
Higher temperature when brainstorming product names — you want variety, not the same five suggestions.
